text: The surgeon begins creating a flap, with the initial dissection appearing routine. As the dissection progresses towards the pubic tubercle, increased scarring becomes visible.
The surgeon approaches the pubic bone area, encountering significant scarring while carefully maintaining dissection close to the interior abdominal wall.

The surgeon carefully dissects near the pubic bone, closely hugging the periosteum to avoid injury to the bladder. The lateral dissection reveals scarred tissue planes and an Ethabon suture, likely from a previous high ligation during a non-mesh hernia repair.

The surgeon works within Bogros's space, a relatively undisturbed anatomical plane, to mobilize the hernia sac. The anatomy appears clearer from this angle, revealing the appendix tucked within the hernia, likely contributing to the patient's symptoms. The vas deferens is also visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully dissects near the bone, attempting to avoid the bladder, which is presumed to be just inferior to the area being manipulated. Hemlock clips from a previous procedure are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon has completely reduced the hernia sac and is parietalizing the cord structures. The search for a small lipoma begins, as indicated by the preoperative CAT scan.
The surgeon performs inferior and medial dissection in a scarred area, preparing for mesh placement. A 16x12 anatomic right ProGrip mesh is positioned to cover the hernia defect adequately.

The surgeon closes the flap and pulls out the inverted hernia sac, revealing a sliding component of the appendix. The angiocath is used to evacuate air from the pocket, marking the end of the procedure.
